_meta.title: NESA Software Engineering 11-12 (2022) — Year 12 Quiz Question Bank

questions.q: Which attack injects malicious SQL code into a web form input to manipulate a database query?
opts: Cross-site scripting (XSS)
SQL injection
Buffer overflow
Man-in-the-middle attack
questions.exp: SQL injection embeds malicious SQL in input fields to manipulate database queries. XSS injects scripts into web pages; buffer overflow exploits memory allocation; MITM intercepts communications.

questions.q: Encryption alone is sufficient to ensure the security of a software application.
questions.exp: Encryption protects data in transit and at rest but cannot prevent injection attacks, broken authentication, or authorisation flaws. Secure applications require multiple overlapping layers of control.

questions.q: Match each security concept to its correct definition.
pairs: Verifying the identity of a user or system
Determining what an authenticated user is permitted to do
Converting data into an unreadable format without a key
Checking that user data meets expected format and type

questions.q: Arrange the secure development lifecycle steps in the correct order.
items: Requirements and threat modelling
Secure design
Secure coding and code review
Security testing
Deployment with security configuration
Ongoing monitoring and patching

questions.q: A ________ is a simulated cyberattack used to identify vulnerabilities before malicious actors can exploit them.
questions.exp: A penetration test is an authorised simulated attack used to discover exploitable vulnerabilities before real attackers find them.

questions.q: Explain what "defence in depth" means in secure software architecture. Give one example of how it could be applied in a student project.
questions.sample: Defence in depth means applying multiple overlapping security controls so that if one is bypassed, others still protect the system. For example, a login system could combine input validation, password hashing, and session expiry.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of hashing a user's password before storing it in a database?
opts: Compress it to save storage space
Make it unrecoverable if the database is breached
Speed up the login comparison process
Allow admins to retrieve it later
questions.exp: Hashing is one-way — it cannot be reversed. If attackers get the database they get hashes, not plaintext passwords.

questions.q: Cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks target the server database directly.
questions.exp: XSS injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users, attacking the browser — not the database. SQL injection targets the database.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a social engineering attack?
opts: Exploiting a buffer overflow in an application
Sending a deceptive email to trick a user into revealing their password
Intercepting unencrypted network traffic
Injecting malicious code into a web form
questions.exp: Social engineering manipulates people rather than systems. Phishing emails trick users into revealing credentials. The other options are technical attacks.

questions.q: HTTPS guarantees that a website is legitimate and trustworthy.
questions.exp: HTTPS only means the connection is encrypted. It does not verify the website is legitimate — attackers can create HTTPS phishing sites.

questions.q: Match each type of malware to its description.
pairs: Encrypts victim files and demands payment for the decryption key
Disguises itself as legitimate software to gain unauthorised access
Records keystrokes to capture passwords and sensitive data
Self-replicates across networks without needing a host file

questions.q: The principle of ________ states that a user or program should only have access to the minimum resources needed to perform their function.
questions.exp: Least privilege limits the damage that can be done if an account is compromised.

questions.q: What does OWASP stand for, and what is its primary purpose?
opts: Open Web Application Security Project — provides resources for improving software security
Online Web Attack Software Program — a hacking toolkit
Open Wireless Access Security Protocol — a Wi-Fi encryption standard
Operational Web Auditing and Scanning Platform — an automated testing tool
questions.exp: OWASP is a non-profit that produces freely available security resources including the OWASP Top 10 list.

questions.q: A firewall can protect a system from attacks that originate from within the organisation's own network.
questions.exp: A traditional perimeter firewall monitors external/internal traffic. Internal firewalls and zero-trust architectures are needed for insider threats.

questions.q: Explain the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption. Give one example of when each would be used.
questions.sample: Symmetric encryption uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt — fast and suitable for large data (e.g., AES). Asymmetric uses a public key to encrypt and private key to decrypt — slower but allows secure key exchange (e.g., RSA in HTTPS handshakes).

questions.q: Arrange these steps of responding to a security breach in the correct order.
items: Detect and confirm the breach has occurred
Contain the breach to prevent further damage
Eradicate the cause (remove malware, patch vulnerability)
Recover systems and restore normal operations
Document and report the incident
Review and improve security controls

questions.q: Which security practice involves regularly checking software dependencies for known vulnerabilities?
opts: Penetration testing
Dependency scanning
Social engineering
Load testing
questions.exp: Dependency scanning automatically checks third-party libraries against databases of known vulnerabilities (CVEs).

questions.q: Two-factor authentication (2FA) eliminates the risk of account compromise entirely.
questions.exp: 2FA significantly reduces risk but does not eliminate it. Attackers can still use SIM swapping, real-time phishing, or social engineering.

questions.q: Match each security testing approach to what it evaluates.
pairs: Simulates real attacks to find exploitable vulnerabilities
Examines source code manually or automatically for security flaws
Automatically identifies known weaknesses across systems and software
Sends random or malformed input to find unexpected failures

questions.q: Which HTTP method is most appropriate for submitting a form that creates a new record on a server?
opts: GET
POST
DELETE
HEAD
questions.exp: POST sends data in the request body to create a new resource. GET retrieves data; DELETE removes resources; HEAD retrieves only headers.

questions.q: CSS is a programming language that controls the logic and behaviour of a web application.
questions.exp: CSS is a stylesheet language for visual presentation. JavaScript controls behaviour and logic. HTML defines structure.

questions.q: Match each web technology to its primary role.
pairs: Defines the structure and content of a web page
Protocol for transferring data between client and server

questions.q: Arrange these events in the order they occur when a user requests a web page.
items: User enters a URL and presses Enter
DNS resolves the domain to an IP address
Browser sends an HTTP GET request to the server
Server processes the request and returns a response
Browser parses HTML and requests linked CSS/JS files
Browser renders the complete page

questions.q: In a client–server model, the ________ processes requests, applies business logic, and returns responses to clients.
questions.exp: The server receives requests, processes them, queries databases if needed, and returns a response.

questions.q: Explain the difference between client-side and server-side processing in a web application. Give one example of each.
questions.sample: Client-side processing runs in the browser — e.g. JavaScript validating a form before submission. Server-side runs on the web server — e.g. a Python script querying a database and returning results.

questions.q: What does an HTTP status code of 404 indicate?
opts: Internal server error
Permanent redirect
Resource not found
Request was successful
questions.exp: 404 = resource not found. 200 = success; 301 = permanent redirect; 500 = internal server error.

questions.q: A RESTful API uses HTTP methods such as GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE to perform operations on resources.
questions.exp: REST maps HTTP verbs to CRUD operations: GET = read, POST = create, PUT/PATCH = update, DELETE = remove.

questions.q: Match each HTTP status code to its meaning.
Which of the following best describes the purpose of a Content Delivery Network (CDN)?
opts: A tool for writing server-side scripts
A distributed network of servers that delivers content from locations close to the user
A database management system for web applications
A security protocol for encrypting web traffic
questions.exp: A CDN caches static assets on servers around the world, reducing latency by serving content geographically close to the user.

questions.q: A cookie stored with the "HttpOnly" flag can be accessed by JavaScript running on the page.
questions.exp: The HttpOnly flag prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie, protecting it from XSS attacks.

questions.q: Match each web development term to its correct description.
pairs: A tree-like representation of an HTML document that can be manipulated with JavaScript
A set of rules that allows different software systems to communicate
A lightweight data interchange format commonly used in web APIs
A technique for sending and receiving data from a server without reloading the page

questions.q: In web development, ________ is the practice of designing pages so they work well on screens of all sizes, from mobile phones to desktop monitors.
questions.exp: Responsive design uses flexible grids, images, and CSS media queries to adapt layouts to different screen sizes.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the DOCTYPE declaration at the top of an HTML file?
opts: To define the page title shown in the browser tab
To tell the browser which version of HTML the document uses
To link an external CSS stylesheet
To specify the character encoding of the file
questions.exp: The DOCTYPE declaration instructs the browser to render the page in standards mode using the specified HTML version.

questions.q: GET requests should be used when the action has side effects, such as creating or deleting data.
questions.exp: GET is for safe, idempotent data retrieval. POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE should be used for operations that modify server state.

questions.q: Describe what a web framework is and explain two advantages of using one when developing a web application.
questions.sample: A web framework provides pre-written code and tools for building web applications (e.g., Django, Flask, Express). Advantages: speeds up development with built-in features like routing and templating; improves security by handling common vulnerabilities automatically.

questions.q: Arrange these steps of a typical web form submission in the correct order.
items: User fills in the form fields
User clicks the Submit button
Client-side JavaScript validates the input
Browser sends an HTTP POST request with form data
Server validates and processes the data
Server returns a response (success or error message)

questions.q: Which HTML element is used to create a hyperlink to another page or resource?
opts: <link>
<href>
<nav>
questions.exp: The <a> (anchor) element creates hyperlinks using the href attribute. <link> links external resources in <head>; href is an attribute; <nav> defines a navigation section.

questions.q: Match each CSS concept to its description.
pairs: A layout model for arranging items in a row or column with flexible sizing
A CSS rule that applies styles based on screen size or device properties
A system that determines which CSS rule takes priority when multiple rules apply
The process by which styles are applied in order of source, specificity, and importance

questions.q: Web accessibility only benefits users with permanent disabilities.
questions.exp: Accessibility benefits everyone: captions help in noisy environments, high contrast helps in bright sunlight, keyboard navigation benefits power users.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes a software bot in the context of automation?
opts: A human who manually tests software
A program that automatically performs repetitive tasks
A type of database management system
A hardware component for parallel processing
questions.exp: A bot automates tasks faster and without rest.

questions.q: Machine learning algorithms always require explicit rules to be programmed for every decision they make.
questions.exp: Machine learning algorithms learn patterns from data rather than following explicitly programmed rules.

questions.q: Match each automation concept to its description.
pairs: A set of instructions executed automatically to complete a task
An interface that allows software systems to communicate and share data
A scheduled task that runs automatically at specified times
An HTTP callback triggered automatically by a specific event in another system

questions.q: Arrange the stages of building a simple automation pipeline in the correct order.
items: Identify the repetitive task and its inputs/outputs
Design the automation logic and data flow
Write and test the automation script
Schedule or trigger the automation
Monitor outputs and handle errors
Refine the automation based on results

questions.q: In software development, ________ integration automatically runs a suite of tests every time new code is committed, to catch regressions immediately.
questions.exp: Continuous integration (CI) triggers automated test suites on each commit.

questions.q: Describe two advantages and one disadvantage of using automation in a software development workflow.
questions.sample: Two advantages: automation executes repetitive tasks faster and more consistently, and frees developers for higher-value work. One disadvantage: automated systems require upfront time and ongoing maintenance.

questions.q: What is the main benefit of using version control (e.g., Git) as part of an automated development workflow?
opts: It speeds up code execution at runtime
It tracks changes and allows rollback to any previous version
It automatically fixes bugs found during testing
It replaces the need for a test suite
questions.exp: Version control records every change with author and date, enabling rollback if a bug is introduced.

questions.q: A webhook and a cron job both wait for a set time interval before executing.
questions.exp: A cron job runs on a fixed schedule. A webhook fires in response to an event — event-driven, not time-driven.

questions.q: In the context of software testing automation, what is a "test suite"?
opts: A collection of related test cases grouped together and run as a unit
A physical room where software testing is conducted
A single test case that checks one specific function
A report generated after manual testing
questions.exp: A test suite groups related test cases to run together automatically, producing a consolidated results report.

questions.q: Continuous deployment (CD) means every code change is automatically deployed to production without any human review.
questions.exp: Most pipelines include automated tests, approval gates, or staged rollouts even when deployment is automated.

questions.q: Match each DevOps term to its correct description.
pairs: An automated workflow that builds, tests, and deploys code changes
A lightweight, portable package that includes an application and its dependencies
Managing and provisioning servers using configuration files instead of manual processes
A system that distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers to prevent overload

questions.q: In automated testing, a ________ test verifies that a new code change has not broken existing functionality that previously worked.
questions.exp: Regression testing re-runs previously passing tests after new changes to ensure nothing has been accidentally broken.

questions.q: Which of the following is the best description of an API in the context of software automation?
opts: A user interface for non-technical users to control software
A set of rules that allows one software system to request services from another
A programming language used to write automation scripts
A database that stores automation logs
questions.exp: An API defines how software systems communicate — what requests can be made, the data format, and what responses to expect.

questions.q: Web scraping is always legal if the data being collected is publicly visible on a website.
questions.exp: Web scraping may breach a site's Terms of Service, copyright law, or privacy laws even if data is publicly visible.

questions.q: Explain what a CI/CD pipeline is and describe two stages it typically includes.
questions.sample: A CI/CD pipeline is an automated workflow from code commit to deployment. Typical stages: build (compiling code), automated testing (running tests), and deployment (pushing to staging or production).

questions.q: Arrange these stages of a typical CI/CD pipeline in the correct order.
items: Developer commits code to version control
CI server detects the commit and triggers the pipeline
Code is built and compiled
Automated tests are run
Build is deployed to a staging environment
Deployment to production (manual approval or automatic)

questions.q: What is the purpose of environment variables in an automated deployment pipeline?
opts: To speed up the execution of scripts
To store configuration values and secrets outside of the codebase
To define the programming language used in the project
To automatically generate documentation
questions.exp: Environment variables store configuration (API keys, database URLs) outside the codebase so sensitive values are not exposed in source code.

questions.q: Match each type of automated test to what it verifies.
pairs: A single function or method in isolation
How multiple modules or systems work together
A complete user workflow from the interface through to the database
How a system behaves under load or stress conditions

questions.q: A cron expression of "0 9 * * 1-5" would trigger a job every weekday at 9:00 AM.
questions.exp: "0 9 * * 1-5" = minute 0, hour 9, any day, any month, Monday–Friday. Fires at 9:00 AM every weekday.

questions.q: According to the NESA syllabus, the first step in the fundamental software development process is:
opts: Design
Testing and debugging
Requirements definition
Development
questions.exp: The eight steps begin with requirements definition, then: determining specifications, design, development, integration, testing and debugging, installation, and maintenance.

questions.q: A non-functional requirement describes a specific behaviour or action the system must perform.
questions.exp: Non-functional requirements describe qualities — performance, security, usability, reliability. Functional requirements describe what the system does.

questions.q: Match each design tool to what it primarily communicates.
pairs: Hierarchical decomposition of a system into modules
How data moves between processes and data stores
Project tasks, durations, and dependencies over time
Visual layout and structure of a user interface

questions.q: Arrange the eight fundamental software development steps in the correct order.
items: Requirements definition
Determining specifications
Design
Development
Integration
Testing and debugging
Installation
Maintenance

questions.q: ________ testing checks input values at the edges of valid and invalid ranges to catch off-by-one errors.
questions.exp: Boundary testing targets exact minimum and maximum valid values, plus values just outside those bounds.

questions.q: Explain the difference between a functional and a non-functional requirement. Give one example of each for a school event booking app.
questions.sample: A functional requirement describes what the system must do. Example: "The system shall allow students to book a seat at a school event." A non-functional requirement describes a quality attribute. Example: "The booking page shall load within two seconds on the school network."

questions.q: An agile development approach differs from a structured (waterfall) approach because agile:
opts: Locks all requirements before any coding begins
Delivers the entire system in one release at the end
Uses iterative cycles with regular client feedback
Requires no design documentation at any stage
questions.exp: Agile uses short iterative sprints with client feedback each cycle. Waterfall locks requirements upfront and delivers in one release.

questions.q: In a test plan, a "fail" result means the developer made an error when writing the test case.
questions.exp: A fail means the actual output did not match the expected output — indicating a defect in the software, not the test.

questions.q: Match each term to its correct description in the context of the Software Engineering Project.
pairs: The system shall send a confirmation email when a booking is made
The system shall support 500 concurrent users without degradation
The project must be completed within 30 hours using only free tools
Students want to cancel a booking without contacting the admin

questions.q: A student's test plan has a test case with an input of 31 days for a booking system that accepts 1–30 days in advance. What type of testing is this, and what is the expected output?
questions.sample: This is boundary testing — testing a value just outside the upper boundary (30 days). The expected output is that the system rejects the booking with an error message.

questions.q: Which of the following is the best definition of a software prototype?
opts: The final, fully tested version of a software product
An early working model used to explore requirements and gather feedback
A document listing all features the system will have
A backup copy of the system kept in case of failure
questions.exp: A prototype is a preliminary working model used to validate requirements, test design ideas, and gather stakeholder feedback.

questions.q: The maintenance phase of the software development lifecycle occurs before installation.
questions.exp: The NESA order is: requirements definition, determining specifications, design, development, integration, testing and debugging, installation, then maintenance.

questions.q: Match each type of testing to its correct description.
pairs: Testing individual modules or functions in isolation
Testing how modules work together as a combined system
Testing conducted by the client or end users to confirm the system meets their needs
Re-running tests after changes to ensure existing functionality still works

questions.q: In a structure chart, a ________ symbol indicates that a module is called multiple times, such as inside a loop.
questions.exp: A small curved arrow or loop symbol indicates the subordinate module is called repeatedly.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) in software design?
opts: To show the order of tasks in a project plan
To model how data moves through a system between processes, stores, and external entities
To display the user interface layout of each screen
To map the hierarchy of modules within the software
questions.exp: A DFD shows how data flows from external entities through processes to data stores, without specifying implementation details.

questions.q: A Gantt chart shows task dependencies — that is, which tasks must be completed before others can begin.
questions.exp: Gantt charts display tasks as horizontal bars and can show dependencies, allowing identification of the critical path.

questions.q: Explain what is meant by the "scope" of a software engineering project and describe two consequences of scope creep.
questions.sample: Scope defines the agreed boundaries of a project. Scope creep occurs when requirements expand beyond the original agreement. Two consequences: late delivery as unplanned work takes longer; reduced quality as developers rush new features without adequate testing.

questions.q: Arrange these steps of writing and executing a test plan in the correct order.
items: Identify the features and functions to be tested
Write individual test cases with inputs and expected outputs
Set up the test environment
Execute each test case and record actual outputs
Compare actual outputs with expected outputs
Document results and report any defects found

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the role of a "client" in the Software Engineering Project?
opts: The end user who will use the finished software every day
The person or organisation that commissions the project and approves the requirements
The teacher who assesses the project submission
The developer who writes the code
questions.exp: The client commissions the software, defines high-level requirements, and approves the final solution. End users may differ from the client.

questions.q: Path testing ensures that every possible route through a program's logic is executed at least once during testing.
questions.exp: Path testing (basis path testing) aims to execute every distinct path through the control flow at least once.

questions.q: Match each project management term to its description.
pairs: A significant checkpoint or deliverable in the project schedule
A relationship where one task cannot start until another is complete
The longest sequence of dependent tasks that determines the minimum project duration
An uncertain event that could have a positive or negative impact on the project

questions.q: The process of checking that a software product meets the requirements specified by the client is called ________.
questions.exp: Validation checks the right product was built. Verification checks the product was built correctly. Both are part of quality assurance.

questions.q: Which of the following are recognised practices for securely storing user passwords in a database? (Select all that apply)
opts: Hashing the password with a strong algorithm like bcrypt or Argon2
Storing the password in plain text for easy retrieval
Adding a unique salt to each password before hashing
Encoding the password in Base64 before storing it
questions.exp: Hashing makes passwords unrecoverable even if the database is breached, and salting prevents rainbow table attacks against common passwords. Plain text storage exposes credentials directly. Base64 is encoding, not security — it is trivially reversible.

questions.q: Which of the following are appropriate ways to prevent cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks on a web form? (Select all that apply)
opts: Escape all user-supplied content when rendering it back to the page
Set the Content-Security-Policy HTTP header to restrict script sources
Use the HttpOnly flag on session cookies
Validate that all submitted input matches expected formats
questions.exp: Escaping output, restricting allowed script sources via CSP, and validating input directly defend against XSS. The HttpOnly flag limits the damage XSS can do by hiding session cookies from JavaScript, but does not prevent XSS itself.

questions.q: Which of the following are typically performed automatically as part of a CI/CD pipeline? (Select all that apply)
opts: Running the project's automated test suite
Manually approving every line of code change
Building and packaging the application
Deploying the application to a staging environment
questions.exp: A CI/CD pipeline automates build, test, and deploy stages. Manual code review may happen separately but is not part of the automation pipeline itself.

questions.q: Which of the following are valid types of test cases that should appear in a comprehensive test plan? (Select all that apply)
opts: Normal cases using typical valid input
Boundary cases at the edges of valid input ranges
Error cases using invalid or unexpected input
Cases that intentionally skip testing to save development time
questions.exp: A comprehensive test plan covers normal, boundary, and error cases — the three categories NESA explicitly references. Skipping tests contradicts the purpose of a test plan.

questions.q: A rainbow table attack works by using a precomputed table that maps common passwords to their hash values. If an attacker steals a database of hashed passwords, they can look up each stolen hash in the table to find the original password. Which technique most effectively defends against this type of attack?
opts: Storing passwords using a longer hashing algorithm such as SHA-512
Adding a unique random salt to each password before hashing
Encrypting the hash values with a secret key
Limiting the number of login attempts per minute
questions.exp: Salting adds a unique random value to each password before hashing, so even users with identical passwords produce different hashes. This makes precomputed rainbow tables useless — an attacker would need a separate table for every possible salt, which is computationally infeasible. A longer hash algorithm alone does not help (the attacker can build a rainbow table for any algorithm). Encrypting the hash adds a layer but introduces key management problems. Rate-limiting login attempts defends against online brute force attacks, not offline rainbow table attacks on a stolen database.

questions.q: A CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) pipeline is an automated workflow that takes code from a developer's commit through to a working version of the software running in production. It is widely used in modern software engineering because it catches problems early and removes manual steps that humans often forget or get wrong. Which of the following are features or purposes of a CI/CD pipeline? (Select all that apply)
opts: Automatically running a suite of tests every time a developer commits new code
Manually transferring files to a production server using FTP
Building and packaging the application into a deployable artefact
Providing fast feedback to developers when their changes break something
Deploying the application to staging or production environments without human intervention each time
questions.exp: A CI/CD pipeline runs automated tests on every commit, builds and packages the application, gives developers fast feedback when something breaks, and automates deployment to staging or production. Manual file transfers via FTP are the opposite of CI/CD — they are slow, error-prone, and rely on humans remembering every step. The whole point of CI/CD is to remove that manual work.

questions.q:
A school library uses a database to track its books. The Books table contains these columns and rows:

BookID | Title              | Author          | Genre       | YearPublished
B001   | The Silver Brumby  | Elyne Mitchell  | Fiction     | 1958
B002   | Possum Magic       | Mem Fox         | Fiction     | 1983
B003   | Australia          | Tim Flannery    | Non-fiction | 2010
B004   | Cloudstreet        | Tim Winton      | Fiction     | 2003
B005   | Bill Bryson Travels| Bill Bryson     | Non-fiction | 2000

A SQL query was run and the result returned was:

Title              | Author
Cloudstreet        | Tim Winton
Possum Magic       | Mem Fox
The Silver Brumby  | Elyne Mitchell

Which of the following are true about the SQL query that produced this result? (Select all that apply)
opts: The query selected the Title and Author columns
The query used SELECT * to return every column
The query included WHERE Genre = 'Fiction'
The query included ORDER BY Title ASC
The query included ORDER BY YearPublished DESC
questions.exp: Only Title and Author appear in the result, so option A is correct and B is wrong (SELECT * would have returned every column). All three rows have Genre = 'Fiction' and the two Non-fiction rows were excluded, so C is correct. The titles are sorted alphabetically (Cloudstreet, Possum Magic, The Silver Brumby), confirming D. If the query had used ORDER BY YearPublished DESC, the order would have been Cloudstreet (2003), Possum Magic (1983), The Silver Brumby (1958) — which happens to match here, but the publication years tell us nothing definitive on their own. The clearest evidence is alphabetical title order, so D is the supported answer and E is not.
